Assessment of one campaign page’s message hierarchy, offer clarity, proof, friction, and primary conversion action.

See also: Website Evaluation, Product Page Evaluation

docs.title: Language Model Tokens
docs.text:
Small units of text, such as words or word fragments, that a language model reads and produces when processing a prompt.

Example: The phrase “budget-friendly” may be split into several units that count against the model’s input limit.

See also: Large Language Models, Model Context Window

docs.title: Lapsed Customer
docs.text:
A former buyer who stopped purchasing and may hold unresolved dissatisfaction, changed needs, or stronger alternatives.

Example: A former subscriber returns only after the company addresses the support failure that caused cancellation.

docs.title: Large Language Models
docs.text:
Generative systems trained on extensive text collections to predict language sequences and perform tasks such as conversation, classification, summarization, and analysis.

Example: A marketer asks a language model to compare persona reactions and summarize the reasons for disagreement.

docs.title: Leading Questions
docs.text:
Questions whose wording or assumptions encourage a particular answer and can distort research findings.

Example: “Don’t you think this guarantee is misleading?” pressures the respondent toward criticism.

docs.title: Logo Evaluation
docs.text: Assessment of a logo’s recognition, distinctiveness, legibility, relevance, versatility, and fit with intended brand meaning.

docs.title: Loyal Customer
docs.text:
A repeat buyer with an established brand preference, prior experience, and greater resistance to competing offers.

Example: A five-year subscriber tolerates a small price change because prior service recovery built trust.

See also: Skeptical Customer, New Customer

docs.title: Market Research
docs.text:
Systematic study of markets, customers, competitors, and demand conditions to support positioning, product, and commercial decisions.

Example: A team compares customer demand, competitor pricing, and category growth before entering a new region.

docs.title: Marketing Asset
docs.text: A brand-controlled item used to communicate or sell, such as a logo, advertisement, website, email, or product page.

docs.title: Media Habits
docs.text:
Recurring patterns in when, where, and how a customer consumes information or entertainment.

Example: The buyer reads industry newsletters each morning and watches product demonstrations on a laptop.

docs.title: Memorability Score
docs.text: A rating of how likely an asset’s distinctive idea or identity is to be remembered after exposure.

docs.title: Message Hierarchy
docs.text:
The deliberate order of primary, supporting, and detailed messages according to what audiences should notice and understand first.

See also: Product Messaging, Messaging Consistency

docs.title: Messaging Consistency
docs.text: Alignment of claims, terminology, emphasis, and tone across channels and stages of the customer journey.

docs.title: Messaging Gap
docs.text: Missing, unclear, or inconsistent information that prevents an audience from understanding the intended message.

docs.title: Minority Opinion
docs.text: A supported interpretation held by fewer reviewers that remains important because agreement alone does not establish truth.

docs.title: Missing Data Handling
docs.text:
Instructions for identifying unavailable evidence and responding without inventing values or unsupported conclusions.

Example: When competitor pricing is absent, the response says “not provided” instead of estimating it.

See also: Citation Field, Prompt Testing

docs.title: Misuse Prevention
docs.text:
Measures that reduce the likelihood that a system, persona, data set, or finding will be applied for harmful purposes.

Example: Access controls prevent a hiring team from repurposing marketing personas to screen applicants.

See also: Harm Assessment, Research Governance

docs.title: Model Bias
docs.text:
Systematic distortion in AI outputs arising from training data, design choices, prompts, evaluation methods, or deployment context.

Example: The model rates identical copy differently after only the customer’s gender-coded name changes.

See also: Hallucination Detection, Automation Bias

docs.title: Model Context Window
docs.text:
The limited amount of tokenized information a language model can consider during one interaction, including instructions, inputs, and prior messages.

Example: A long interview transcript is shortened because the prompt, transcript, and requested response exceed the model’s available input space.

docs.title: Model Hallucination
docs.text:
Plausible-sounding content generated without adequate factual support in the supplied evidence or reliable knowledge.

Example: The reviewer invents an award that never appears in the supplied product brief.

docs.title: Model Instructions
docs.text:
Directions supplied to an AI system that define its role, task, constraints, priorities, or expected form of response.

Example: The configuration says, “Use only supplied evidence, report uncertainty, and return the approved evaluation fields.”

docs.title: Model Responses
docs.text:
The text or other content produced by an AI model after it processes instructions and contextual information.

Example: The saved answer contains a clarity rating, cited page text, confidence level, and recommendation.

docs.title: Moderated Discussion
docs.text: A conversation guided by a neutral facilitator who manages questions, participation, focus, and summary.

docs.title: Moderator Agent
docs.text:
A neutral facilitator agent that asks questions, manages participation, and summarizes discussion without deciding the preferred answer.

See also: Persona Agent, Expert Reviewer Agent

docs.title: Moderator Neutrality
docs.text: Facilitation that avoids favoring a conclusion, participant, brand, or interpretation during discussion and synthesis.

docs.title: Moderator Prompt
docs.text:
Instructions that tell a facilitator agent how to ask questions, manage participation, remain neutral, and summarize discussion.

Example: The facilitator is told to invite each persona once, probe disagreement, and summarize without taking sides.

docs.title: Moment of Truth
docs.text:
A touchpoint with unusually strong influence on trust, satisfaction, or the decision to continue the relationship.

Example: The first support response after a failed migration determines whether the customer continues.

docs.title: Multi-Agent System
docs.text: A coordinated arrangement of multiple AI participants with distinct roles, contexts, or tasks that contribute to one workflow.

docs.title: Needs-Based Segmentation
docs.text:
Grouping customers by the problems, required benefits, or desired outcomes they are trying to address.

Example: Buyers are grouped by migration help, compliance proof, collaboration, or lowest total cost.

docs.title: Negative Persona
docs.text:
A profile of someone the offering should not target, used to prevent wasted effort and misleading broad appeal.

Example: Students seeking a free personal tool are excluded from a campaign for enterprise software.

docs.title: Neutral Question Design
docs.text:
Writing questions that do not suggest a preferred answer or imply that one response is more acceptable.

Example: The moderator asks, “What stands out about the price?” rather than implying that it is confusing.

docs.title: New Customer
docs.text:
A recent or prospective buyer who lacks direct experience with the brand and needs orientation, proof, and reassurance.

Example: A first-time visitor needs category explanation and proof that existing customers no longer need.

docs.title: No-Code Workflow
docs.text: A process assembled through visual interfaces and configuration rather than conventional software programming.

docs.title: Open-Ended Questions
docs.text:
Questions that permit respondents to answer in their own words rather than choose from fixed options.

Example: “Tell me how you would decide between these plans” permits an answer in the customer’s own terms.

docs.title: Opinion Contamination
docs.text:
A change in an independent judgment caused by seeing another participant’s view before recording one’s own.

See also: Groupthink Risk, Persona Cross-Talk

docs.title: Organizational Memory
docs.text: A durable, searchable record of findings, evidence, decisions, and lessons that remains available beyond one project or team.

docs.title: Output Instructions
docs.text:
Requirements that define the response’s format, fields, length, ordering, data types, and any content that must be included or omitted.

Example: The response must return one JSON object with rating, evidence, confidence, and recommendation fields.

See also: Evaluation Instructions, Prompt Specificity

docs.title: Output Schema
docs.text:
The specified fields, data types, and arrangement required in a structured response.

Example: The schema requires strings for evidence and rationale plus integers from one through five for ratings.

See also: Structured Output, Response Format

docs.title: Pain Point Node
docs.text: A graph node representing a documented customer problem and linking it to evidence, journey stages, and recommendations.

docs.title: Parallel Evaluation
docs.text: Independent assessments performed at the same stage without one reviewer seeing another reviewer’s response first.

docs.title: Pattern Detection
docs.text: The systematic identification of repeated behaviors, reactions, reasons, or relationships across evaluation responses.

docs.title: Persona Acceptance Test
docs.text:
The pass-or-fail check that determines whether a profile meets stated readiness criteria.

Example: The Acceptance Test field records: “fails the profile when it recommends a product that violates its stated budget.”

docs.title: Persona Agent
docs.text: An agent instructed to evaluate an asset from one defined customer profile while preserving that profile’s viewpoint.

docs.title: Persona Attitudes
docs.text:
The learned positive, negative, or mixed evaluations of relevant subjects.

Example: The Attitudes field records: “welcomes automation but distrusts unexplained recommendations.”

docs.title: Persona Background
docs.text:
The relevant life, work, and market history that shapes present choices.

Example: The Background field records: “five years managing purchases for a small clinic.”

docs.title: Persona Balance
docs.text:
The proportionate representation of relevant viewpoints without letting one type dominate.

Example: The Balance field records: “contains two cautious and two adventurous buyers instead of six enthusiasts.”

See also: Persona Diversity, Persona Refinement

docs.title: Persona Behaviors
docs.text:
The observable actions and routines relevant to the buying situation.

Example: The Behaviors field records: “reads comparison pages and asks a peer before buying.”

docs.title: Persona Beliefs
docs.text:
The ideas the represented customer accepts as true about a product, market, or situation.

Example: The Beliefs field records: “switching software will disrupt her team for several weeks.”

docs.title: Persona Benchmark
docs.text:
The reference profile result used to compare later versions or model configurations.

Example: The Benchmark field records: “compares the revised profile with the approved v1.2 responses.”

See also: Persona Acceptance Test, Prompt Engineering

docs.title: Persona Comparison Report
docs.text: A report that contrasts how defined personas responded, where they agreed, and why important differences appeared.

docs.title: Persona Completeness
docs.text:
The coverage of the fields needed for the intended evaluation.

Example: The Completeness field records: “includes goals, barriers, context, criteria, and evidence.”

See also: Persona Specificity, Customer Segmentation

docs.title: Persona Consistency
docs.text:
The alignment among a profile’s traits, circumstances, and responses across tasks.

Example: The Consistency field records: “rejects unsupported claims in both the website and email tests.”

docs.title: Persona Context
docs.text:
The situation in which the represented customer encounters the tested asset.

Example: The Context field records: “comparing plans on a phone during her commute.”

See also: Persona Narrative, Persona Demographics

docs.title: Persona Coverage
docs.text:
The extent to which the profile set represents the important customer situations in scope.

Example: The Coverage field records: “adds a mobile, low-connectivity buying situation missing from the set.”

docs.title: Persona Cross-Talk
docs.text: Direct interaction among persona agents that can reveal reactions but may also blur their independent viewpoints.

docs.title: Persona Demographics
docs.text:
The observable population attributes such as age range, location, or household type.

Example: The Demographics field records: “age 42, Chicago, two-person household.”

docs.title: Persona Differentiation
docs.text:
The meaningful separation between this profile and other profiles in the study.

Example: The Differentiation field records: “prioritizes compliance while the second persona prioritizes setup speed.”

See also: Persona Quality, Persona Overlap

docs.title: Persona Disclosure
docs.text:
A clear statement that a profile or response is synthetic, including its source, purpose, and relevant limitations.

Example: The Disclosure field records: “a concrete value relevant to the current buying decision.”

docs.title: Persona Diversity
docs.text:
The meaningful variety of circumstances, needs, and viewpoints across the profile set.

Example: The Diversity field records: “includes distinct needs and circumstances rather than cosmetic demographic changes.”

docs.title: Persona Documentation
docs.text:
The record of a profile’s sources, assumptions, fields, versions, and intended uses.

Example: The Documentation field records: “links every profile claim to a source or assumption label.”

docs.title: Persona Drift
docs.text:
The unintended movement away from the profile’s defined traits or viewpoint.

Example: The Drift field records: “begins praising luxury features despite being defined as budget constrained.”

See also: Character Fidelity, Drift Detection

docs.title: Persona Evidence Base
docs.text:
The documented research sources and assumptions supporting the profile’s characteristics.

Example: The Evidence Base field records: “six interviews and 74 coded support tickets.”

docs.title: Persona Frustrations
docs.text:
The recurring sources of annoyance or dissatisfaction during a task or journey.

Example: The Frustrations field records: “support answers arrive after the purchasing deadline.”

See also: Persona Pain Points, Emotional Drivers

docs.title: Persona Goals
docs.text:
The results the represented customer is actively trying to achieve.

Example: The Goals field records: “select a replacement tool before Friday.”

See also: Persona Beliefs, Persona Motivations

docs.title: Persona Identity
docs.text:
The recognizable combination of traits that makes the represented customer distinct.

Example: The Identity field records: “Maya, a cautious operations manager.”

docs.title: Persona Library
docs.text:
The organized collection of approved profiles available for discovery and reuse.

Example: The Library field records: “lists approved profiles by market, purpose, owner, and version.”

docs.title: Persona Motivations
docs.text:
The internal or external reasons that energize a choice or action.

Example: The Motivations field records: “avoid downtime and look prepared to leadership.”

docs.title: Persona Narrative
docs.text:
The brief story connecting the profile’s circumstances, needs, and behavior.

Example: The Narrative field records: “she needs a compliant tool before her current contract expires.”

docs.title: Persona Needs
docs.text:
The conditions or benefits required to resolve a problem or make progress.

Example: The Needs field records: “clear migration steps and predictable total cost.”

docs.title: Persona Node
docs.text:
A knowledge-graph record representing one persona and linking its profile, evaluations, reactions, and evidence.

See also: Graph-Based Evaluation, Asset Node

docs.title: Persona Overlap
docs.text:
The shared characteristics that may cause two profiles to produce similar perspectives.

Example: The Overlap field records: “shares price concern with two other profiles.”

docs.title: Persona Pain Points
docs.text:
The specific problems or obstacles that create difficulty in the customer experience.

Example: The Pain Points field records: “hidden fees appear only at checkout.”

docs.title: Persona Preferences
docs.text:
The options or experiences the represented customer tends to favor.

Example: The Preferences field records: “prefers transparent monthly pricing and live support.”

See also: Persona Behaviors, Persona Attitudes

docs.title: Persona Profile
docs.text:
The complete structured record of the customer representation.

Example: The Profile field records: “a concrete value relevant to the current buying decision.”

See also: Persona Template, Persona Identity

docs.title: Persona Prompt
docs.text:
Instructions that define a persona’s profile, situation, behavioral rules, evidence limits, and response style for a simulation.

Example: The Prompt field records: “a concrete value relevant to the current buying decision.”

docs.title: Persona Psychographics
docs.text:
The interests, values, attitudes, and lifestyles that help explain preferences.

Example: The Psychographics field records: “values independence and dislikes status-driven purchases.”

docs.title: Persona Purpose
docs.text:
The research question and business decision the profile is intended to inform.

Example: The Purpose field records: “decide whether the page reassures first-time buyers.”

docs.title: Persona Quality
docs.text:
The overall fitness of a profile for its stated research purpose.

Example: The Quality field records: “passes evidence, completeness, differentiation, and consistency checks.”

docs.title: Persona Quote
docs.text:
The short first-person statement that captures characteristic language without pretending to be verbatim evidence.

Example: The Quote field records: ““Show me the full cost before I create an account.”.”

See also: Trust Disposition, Persona Scenario

docs.title: Persona Realism
docs.text:
The degree to which a persona’s characteristics and responses form a plausible, evidence-grounded representation of a customer.

Example: The Realism field records: “a concrete value relevant to the current buying decision.”

See also: Simulation Fidelity, Response Variability

docs.title: Persona Refinement
docs.text:
The evidence-based revision that makes a profile clearer, more realistic, or more useful.

Example: The Refinement field records: “replaces an assumed preference with wording found in interviews.”

docs.title: Persona Reuse
docs.text:
The application of an existing profile to another compatible question after checking fit.

Example: The Reuse field records: “uses the profile again only after confirming the new product has the same decision context.”

See also: Persona Library, Persona Documentation

docs.title: Persona Scenario
docs.text:
The specific situation used to place the represented customer in a realistic decision context.

Example: The Scenario field records: “her current contract expires in 30 days.”

docs.title: Persona Specificity
docs.text:
The level of concrete, decision-relevant detail in a profile.

Example: The Specificity field records: “names the decision, deadline, budget, and proof required.”

docs.title: Persona Template
docs.text:
The standard set of fields used to build comparable customer profiles.

Example: The Template field records: “a concrete value relevant to the current buying decision.”

docs.title: Persona Test Case
docs.text:
The defined situation and expected behavioral checks used to examine profile performance.

Example: The Test Case field records: “asks the profile to identify an unsupported guarantee and cite the text.”

docs.title: Persona Validation
docs.text:
The comparison of a profile and its outputs with source evidence and real-customer findings.

Example: The Validation field records: “matches its predicted concerns against five customer interviews.”

docs.title: Persona Values
docs.text:
The enduring principles used to judge what matters or feels acceptable.

Example: The Values field records: “privacy and reliability matter more than novelty.”

docs.title: Persona Versioning
docs.text:
The recording successive profile revisions so changes and results remain traceable.

Example: The Versioning field records: “stores v1.3 with its changed risk tolerance and test results.”

docs.title: Personal Data
docs.text:
Information that identifies a person directly or can reasonably be linked with other information to identify that person.

Example: An email address and purchase history can be linked to one buyer and therefore require protection.

docs.title: Persuasiveness Score
docs.text: A rating of how effectively an asset combines relevance, evidence, emotion, and action to influence a decision.

docs.title: Premium Customer
docs.text:
A buyer willing to pay more for superior quality, service, exclusivity, performance, or reduced risk.

Example: A buyer selects the higher-priced plan for priority support, audit logs, and reduced implementation risk.

See also: Budget Customer, Jobs-to-Be-Done

docs.title: Price Sensitivity
docs.text:
The degree to which price changes affect a customer’s interest, choice, or willingness to buy.

Example: A $10 monthly increase moves the budget persona from the preferred plan to a competitor.

See also: Technology Comfort, Risk Tolerance

docs.title: Primary Persona
docs.text:
The customer profile representing the audience whose needs most strongly guide the evaluated design or message.

Example: The redesign chiefly serves Maya, the clinic manager who owns the purchase decision.

See also: Customer Archetypes, Secondary Persona

docs.title: Probabilistic Output
docs.text:
A result selected from possible continuations according to modeled likelihoods, causing reasonable responses to vary across otherwise similar runs.

Example: The same persona rates a headline 3 on one run and 4 on another, although both explanations identify unclear pricing.

See also: Model Responses, Synthetic Users

docs.title: Probing Questions
docs.text:
Follow-up questions that seek greater detail, reasoning, evidence, or clarification about an earlier response.

Example: After “the price worries me,” the moderator asks, “Which part creates that concern?”

See also: Open-Ended Questions, Adversarial Questions

docs.title: Product Messaging
docs.text: The coordinated claims and explanations used to communicate a product’s audience, benefits, features, proof, and differentiation.

docs.title: Product Page Evaluation
docs.text: Assessment of whether a product page supplies the benefits, specifications, proof, price, and purchase guidance customers need.

docs.title: Prompt Benchmark
docs.text:
A stable set of test cases and reference results used to compare prompt or model versions.

Example: Twenty unchanged cases provide a stable comparison for prompt v3 and prompt v4.

docs.title: Prompt Chaining
docs.text:
A workflow in which one prompt’s output becomes controlled input to a later prompt.

Example: One prompt codes individual reactions; its structured output feeds a second prompt that synthesizes themes.

docs.title: Prompt Clarity
docs.text:
The degree to which instructions have one understandable interpretation and use direct, consistent language.

Example: The team replaces “make it better somehow” with one direct task and an explicit success condition.

docs.title: Prompt Comparison
docs.text:
A controlled test of two or more prompts on the same cases using the same evaluation criteria.

Example: Two prompt versions evaluate the same assets with the same model, settings, and rubric.

See also: Prompt Benchmark, Prompt Iteration

docs.title: Prompt Context
docs.text:
Background information and source material supplied in a prompt so the model can interpret the task correctly.

Example: The prompt includes the product brief, pricing page, persona profile, and study objective before the task.

docs.title: Prompt Engineering
docs.text:
The deliberate design and testing of instructions, context, examples, and output requirements to improve an AI system’s task performance.

Example: A team adds a role, evidence rule, output schema, and test cases, then keeps only revisions that improve the benchmark.

docs.title: Prompt Examples
docs.text:
Sample inputs, behaviors, or outputs included to show how instructions should be applied.

Example: A sample shows an acceptable finding with a quoted sentence, rating, confidence, and bounded recommendation.

See also: Prompt Grounding, Few-Shot Examples

docs.title: Prompt Failure Mode
docs.text:
A recurring way a prompt produces an incorrect, inconsistent, unsafe, or unusable response.

Example: The model repeatedly gives a rating without evidence whenever the source text is long.

docs.title: Prompt Grounding
docs.text:
Requiring a response to rely on supplied evidence or identified sources rather than unsupported model-generated claims.

Example: The reviewer is required to quote the supplied page before asserting that pricing is unclear.

docs.title: Prompt Iteration
docs.text:
A cycle of revising a prompt, testing it, reviewing failures, and retaining changes supported by results.

Example: After a missed citation, the team revises the evidence rule and reruns the failed case.

docs.title: Prompt Objective
docs.text:
The single outcome a prompt is intended to produce, stated clearly enough to judge whether the response succeeds.

Example: The instruction begins, “Identify the two strongest barriers to signup.”

docs.title: Prompt Specificity
docs.text:
The amount of concrete detail that narrows a prompt’s task, audience, evidence, constraints, and expected response.

Example: “Review this” becomes “Rate headline clarity for first-time buyers on a five-point anchored scale.”

docs.title: Prompt Structure
docs.text:
The ordered arrangement of role, task, context, constraints, examples, and output directions within an instruction set.

Example: The saved prompt contains labeled Role, Task, Context, Constraints, and Output sections in that order.

docs.title: Prompt Templates
docs.text:
Standard prompt structures containing fixed instructions and clearly marked fields for variable content.

Example: A locked instruction block surrounds editable fields for audience, asset, question, and rubric.

See also: Reusable Prompts, Prompt Chaining

docs.title: Prompt Test Case
docs.text:
A documented input, context, expected behavior, and pass condition used to evaluate a prompt.

Example: One case supplies an unsupported guarantee and passes only if the model flags and quotes it.

docs.title: Prompt Testing
docs.text:
Running defined cases against a prompt to find failures and measure whether revisions improve performance.

Example: The team runs ten fixed cases after every revision and records pass or fail for each requirement.

docs.title: Prompt Variables
docs.text:
Named placeholders whose values change between runs while the surrounding prompt remains stable.

Example: The template substitutes {{persona}}, {{asset}}, and {{rubric}} for each evaluation run.

docs.title: Prompt Versioning
docs.text:
Recording prompt revisions and their results so teams can reproduce, compare, and restore tested instructions.

Example: The log links prompt v2.4 to its changed evidence rule and benchmark results.

docs.title: Psychographic Segmentation
docs.text:
Grouping customers by values, attitudes, interests, identity, or lifestyle rather than observable demographics alone.

Example: One group prioritizes independence and novelty; another values predictability and expert guidance.

See also: Demographic Segmentation, Needs-Based Segmentation

docs.title: Pull Forces
docs.text:
Attractive benefits and anticipated progress that draw a customer toward a new solution.

Example: A guided migration service and transparent pricing attract the buyer to the new vendor.

docs.title: Purchase Barriers
docs.text:
Concerns or obstacles that delay, prevent, or redirect a customer’s buying decision.

Example: An annual commitment, unclear cancellation terms, and missing security evidence delay signup.

docs.title: Purchase Intent
docs.text: A customer’s stated likelihood or willingness to buy, treated as an attitude rather than proof of future behavior.

docs.title: Purchase Triggers
docs.text:
Events or conditions that move a customer from passive interest toward an active buying decision.

Example: An expiring contract and a limited migration window prompt the team to begin vendor selection.

docs.title: Push Forces
docs.text:
Dissatisfaction or changed circumstances that make a customer’s current solution less acceptable.

Example: Repeated outages make the current scheduling tool unacceptable.

docs.title: Qualitative Research
docs.text:
Research that examines meanings, motivations, language, and experiences through nonnumeric evidence such as interviews, observations, and open responses.

Example: Eight interviews reveal that customers describe the onboarding process as “uncertain” rather than merely “slow.”

docs.title: Quantitative Research
docs.text:
Research that measures variables numerically and analyzes patterns using counts, percentages, comparisons, or statistical methods.

Example: A survey finds that 38 percent of 500 respondents recognize the new tagline.

See also: Qualitative Research, Traditional Focus Groups

docs.title: Rating Scale
docs.text:
An ordered set of response choices used to represent different levels of a measured quality.

See also: Scoring System, Scale Anchors

docs.title: Rating Scale Output
docs.text:
A structured response that records a selected rating together with its scale and supporting rationale.

Example: The record stores clarity: 2, the anchor “frequent confusion,” and supporting page text.

docs.title: Rationale Field
docs.text:
A structured-output location explaining why a rating, finding, or recommendation follows from the evidence.

Example: The explanation connects the low trust rating to an unexplained renewal condition in the asset.

docs.title: Reaction Node
docs.text:
A graph node representing a persona’s recorded emotional, cognitive, or behavioral response to an asset.

See also: Pain Point Node, Criterion Node

docs.title: Real Customer Validation
docs.text:
Testing a synthetic finding with appropriate evidence from actual customers before treating it as decision-ready.

Example: Five recruited buyers confirm that the pricing label identified by synthetic personas is confusing.

See also: Synthetic Data, Evidence Triangulation

docs.title: Recommendation
docs.text:
A proposed action tied to a documented finding, intended outcome, and supporting evidence.

See also: Risk Likelihood, Recommendation Priority

docs.title: Recommendation Field
docs.text:
A structured-output location for an action proposed in response to a documented finding.

Example: The response proposes moving the total monthly price beside the primary signup button.

docs.title: Recommendation List
docs.text:
An ordered collection of proposed actions, each connected to evidence, expected impact, priority, and ownership.

See also: Evaluation Heat Map, Improvement Plan

docs.title: Recommendation Node
docs.text: A graph node representing a proposed action and linking it to supporting findings, priority, effort, and expected impact.

docs.title: Recommendation Priority
docs.text: The relative order in which proposed actions should be considered based on impact, urgency, evidence, risk, and effort.

docs.title: Reflection Prompt
docs.text:
An instruction asking an agent to reconsider its reasoning, evidence, assumptions, or consistency before finalizing.

Example: Before finalizing, the reviewer checks whether every claim has evidence and whether the persona remained consistent.

docs.title: Relationship Type
docs.text:
A controlled label that defines what a graph edge means, such as SUPPORTS, EVALUATES, REACTS_TO, or ADDRESSES.

See also: Evidence Node, Organizational Memory

docs.title: Relevance Score
docs.text: A rating of how closely an asset addresses the intended audience’s situation, priorities, and desired outcomes.

docs.title: Repeatable Evaluation
docs.text:
An assessment whose documented inputs, prompts, criteria, and procedures can be applied again in a consistent way.

Example: The saved asset, prompt version, model setting, rubric, and run procedure allow the test to be run again.

docs.title: Reproducible Results
docs.text:
Findings that can be obtained again by another evaluator using the documented materials, settings, and procedure.

Example: A second analyst follows the documented procedure and obtains the same leading themes and similar scores.

docs.title: Research Assumptions
docs.text:
Conditions treated as true for planning or interpretation even though they may not have been directly established by evidence.

Example: The team records its unverified belief that visitors already understand the product category.

docs.title: Research Constraints
docs.text:
Limits on a study imposed by available time, budget, data, tools, access, ethics, or method.

Example: The study has one week, no customer email list, and a maximum recruitment budget of $3,000.

docs.title: Research Cost
docs.text:
The money, labor, tools, incentives, and opportunity costs required to plan, conduct, analyze, and report a study.

Example: The budget includes recruitment, incentives, moderator time, transcription, software, and analysis.

docs.title: Research Governance
docs.text:
Roles, standards, approvals, documentation, and controls that make research quality, ethics, and accountability manageable.

Example: A policy names the study owner, approval steps, evidence standard, retention period, and escalation contact.

docs.title: Research Objectives
docs.text:
Clear statements of what a study intends to learn, evaluate, compare, or decide.

Example: The brief states that the study will identify the two strongest barriers to trial signup.

docs.title: Research Questions
docs.text:
Specific questions a study is designed to answer through evidence rather than assumption or preference.

Example: “Which proof points make first-time buyers trust the guarantee?” directs the study toward a specific answer.

docs.title: Research Reliability
docs.text:
The degree to which a method produces sufficiently consistent measurements or findings when repeated under comparable conditions.

Example: Two trained reviewers applying the same coding guide assign nearly all comments to the same themes.

See also: Research Validity, Repeatable Evaluation

docs.title: Research Sample Size
docs.text:
The number of participants, observations, or simulated profiles included in a study and considered when interpreting its evidence.

Example: The report states that 12 interviews support qualitative themes but not population estimates.

See also: Customer Surveys, Research Turnaround Time

docs.title: Research Scope
docs.text:
The defined boundaries of a study, including included audiences, materials, questions, contexts, and time period.

Example: The study covers U.S. first-time buyers, the mobile landing page, and the signup decision, but not retention.

See also: Research Objectives, Research Assumptions

docs.title: Research Transparency
docs.text:
Clear disclosure of a study’s purpose, methods, sources, assumptions, limitations, and use of AI.

Example: The report lists the model, prompt version, synthetic sample, assumptions, and validation status.

See also: Ethical AI Use, Informed Consent

docs.title: Research Turnaround Time
docs.text:
The elapsed time from defining a study through collecting, analyzing, and communicating its findings.

Example: The team delivers a concept-test report four business days after receiving the final assets.

docs.title: Research Validity
docs.text:
The degree to which evidence and methods support the interpretation or decision a study claims to support.

Example: A checkout test measures whether customers can complete checkout, not whether they remember the brand a week later.

docs.title: Response Aggregation
docs.text: Mechanical or analytical combination of multiple responses into counts, distributions, summaries, or composite results.

docs.title: Response Coding
docs.text: The assignment of consistent labels to portions of qualitative responses so similar evidence can be grouped and analyzed.

docs.title: Response Consistency
docs.text:
The degree to which repeated outputs follow the same role, criteria, format, and reasoning expectations under comparable inputs.

Example: Across five runs, the persona identifies the same barrier and uses the required fields despite wording changes.

docs.title: Response Format
docs.text:
The presentation form required for an answer, such as a table, list, narrative, or structured record.

Example: The executive result is requested as a two-column table followed by three prioritized actions.

docs.title: Response Variability
docs.text:
Differences among outputs produced from the same or similar inputs because language model generation is probabilistic.

Example: Across five runs, a persona uses different wording but repeatedly flags the same hidden renewal condition.

docs.title: Responsible AI
docs.text:
The design and use of AI with attention to accuracy, fairness, privacy, transparency, safety, and human accountability.

Example: The team minimizes personal data, tests for unfair patterns, discloses simulation, and requires human approval.

docs.title: Reusable Prompts
docs.text:
Tested instructions written with stable language and replaceable inputs for repeated use across compatible evaluations.

Example: The same tested instruction evaluates five landing pages by changing only approved variables.

docs.title: Reviewer Independence
docs.text: Protection of each reviewer’s initial judgment from pressure, imitation, and prior exposure to others’ conclusions.

docs.title: Reviewer Prompt
docs.text:
Instructions that tell an evaluator what asset to assess, which criteria to apply, and what evidence to report.

Example: The reviewer must score the landing page, cite exact wording, and propose one evidence-linked revision.

See also: Moderator Prompt, Skeptic Prompt

docs.title: Risk Assessment
docs.text: A structured examination of what could go wrong, whom it could affect, how likely it is, and how serious it would be.

docs.title: Risk Likelihood
docs.text: The estimated chance that an identified risk will occur under stated conditions.

docs.title: Risk Severity
docs.text: The magnitude of harm or negative business consequence if an identified risk occurs.

docs.title: Risk Tolerance
docs.text:
The amount of uncertainty or possible loss a customer accepts when considering an unfamiliar choice.

Example: The cautious buyer refuses a new vendor without references and a reversible monthly contract.

docs.title: Role Definition
docs.text:
A clear statement of the perspective, expertise, authority, and responsibilities assigned within a prompt.

Example: The instruction says, “You are a neutral moderator, not a brand advocate or final decision-maker.”

docs.title: Round-Table Discussion
docs.text:
A structured exchange in which several agents compare perspectives after completing their initial independent reviews.

See also: Independent Review, Moderated Discussion

docs.title: Rubric Completeness
docs.text: The degree to which a rubric covers every criterion needed to answer the stated evaluation objective.

docs.title: Rubric Dimension
docs.text:
A distinct aspect of performance that an evaluation rubric measures, such as clarity, trust, or relevance.

See also: Evaluation Rubric, Rubric Completeness

docs.title: Rubric Fairness
docs.text: The degree to which rubric criteria and scoring avoid irrelevant disadvantage, stereotypes, and unequal standards.

docs.title: Scalable Research Process
docs.text:
A documented method that can handle more assets, personas, or studies without proportional growth in cost, time, or inconsistency.

See also: Capstone System

docs.title: Scale Anchors
docs.text: Plain-language descriptions of what specific points on a rating scale mean and what evidence supports them.

docs.title: Score Normalization
docs.text:
The conversion of scores from different scales or distributions into a common form for valid comparison.

See also: Weighted Criteria, Confidence Rating

docs.title: Scoring Guidance
docs.text: Instructions that explain how reviewers should choose ratings, handle uncertainty, and cite supporting observations.

docs.title: Scoring System
docs.text: A defined method for converting evidence-based judgments into comparable numbers, categories, or ordered ratings.

docs.title: Secondary Persona
docs.text:
A relevant customer profile considered after the primary audience because its needs are important but less central.

Example: The accountant’s review needs remain important but do not override the clinic manager’s core workflow.

docs.title: Selection Bias
docs.text:
Systematic distortion caused when the included people, personas, examples, or evidence do not adequately represent the intended population.

Example: Testing only loyal subscribers makes the new-customer onboarding page appear easier than it is.

docs.title: Sensitive Attributes
docs.text:
Personal characteristics such as health, race, religion, disability, or sexuality that can create heightened privacy or discrimination risks.

Example: A health condition is excluded from the persona because it is unnecessary for testing the pricing message.

See also: Personal Data, Data Minimization

docs.title: Sentiment Pattern
docs.text: A recurring distribution or change in positive, negative, or mixed reactions across audiences, assets, or journey stages.

docs.title: Sequential Evaluation
docs.text: Assessments performed in an ordered series where a later step may use an earlier step’s result.

docs.title: Shared Context
docs.text: Information deliberately made available to multiple agents so they work from the same relevant facts and materials.

docs.title: Simulation Fidelity
docs.text:
The degree to which a simulation preserves the relevant characteristics and behavior of the real situation it represents.

Example: A checkout simulation includes the real price, device layout, and cancellation terms because those details affect the decision.

docs.title: Skeptic Agent
docs.text: An agent tasked with challenging claims, evidence, and assumptions to reveal weaknesses that agreeable reviewers may miss.

docs.title: Skeptic Prompt
docs.text:
Instructions that direct an agent to challenge claims, inspect risks, and identify unsupported assumptions without becoming indiscriminately negative.

Example: The skeptic is instructed to challenge guarantees, missing proof, hidden conditions, and optimistic assumptions.

docs.title: Skeptical Customer
docs.text:
A customer who requires strong evidence and actively questions claims, motives, risks, and hidden conditions before accepting an offer.

Example: The buyer refuses “industry leading” until the page supplies an independent source and comparison method.

docs.title: Social Content Evaluation
docs.text: Assessment of social posts for platform fit, clarity, authenticity, engagement value, brand voice, and response risk.

docs.title: Social Job
docs.text:
The way a customer wants to be perceived by others or relate to a relevant group.

Example: The manager wants leadership to see the purchase as careful and competent.

docs.title: Social Needs
docs.text:
Desired effects on belonging, recognition, status, or relationships that influence a customer’s choice.

Example: The manager wants colleagues to view her recommendation as careful and professionally credible.

docs.title: Stereotype Risk
docs.text:
The possibility that a persona or output reduces a group to oversimplified traits and produces unfair or misleading conclusions.

Example: A profile assumes every retired customer dislikes technology without supporting research.

docs.title: Structured Output
docs.text:
A response organized into named fields or a fixed schema so results can be checked and compared.

Example: Every review returns the same four named fields instead of an unstructured paragraph.

docs.title: Supporting Evidence
docs.text: Specific recorded information that directly justifies a finding, rating, risk, or recommendation.

docs.title: Switching Forces
docs.text:
The combined pressures that push customers from a current solution, pull them toward another, or hold them back.

Example: Poor support pushes the buyer away, easier migration pulls her forward, and contract anxiety holds her back.

See also: Customer Progress, Push Forces

docs.title: Synthesis Prompt
docs.text:
An instruction asking an agent to combine multiple findings into a coherent account while preserving important differences.

Example: The synthesizer combines shared pricing concerns while preserving one persona’s contrary view and its evidence.

See also: Reflection Prompt, AI Agent

docs.title: Synthetic Data
docs.text:
Artificially generated records designed to resemble useful properties of real data without necessarily describing actual individuals.

Example: Artificial purchase records preserve broad spending patterns without reproducing any customer’s actual history.

docs.title: Synthetic Personas
docs.text:
Structured, AI-operable customer profiles that combine relevant traits, goals, circumstances, and behaviors for repeatable simulated evaluation.

Example: A profile combines a price-sensitive owner’s goals, constraints, purchase criteria, and media habits for repeated tests.

docs.title: Synthetic User Research
docs.text:
Structured inquiry using simulated customer representations to produce early directional findings that remain subject to validation with real people.

Example: Simulated reactions reveal a possible trust problem that the team adds to its next real-customer interview guide.

See also: AI Persona Testing, Customer Simulation

docs.title: Synthetic Users
docs.text:
AI-generated representations of people used to explore possible customer reactions without claiming that the representations are real research participants.

Example: Twenty simulated shoppers review an early concept before any claim is tested with recruited customers.

docs.title: System Prompt
docs.text:
High-priority instructions that establish an AI system’s role, operating rules, and behavior across an interaction.

Example: A high-priority instruction states, “Act as a neutral research moderator and never invent customer evidence.”

See also: Prompt Structure, User Prompt

docs.title: Tagline Evaluation
docs.text: Assessment of a tagline’s clarity, memorability, distinctiveness, credibility, adaptability, and connection to the brand.

docs.title: Task Definition
docs.text:
A clear statement of the work to perform, including the object of analysis and desired result.

Example: The task reads, “Find wording that could make cancellation terms difficult to understand.”

See also: Role Definition, Audience Definition

docs.title: Technology Comfort
docs.text:
A customer’s readiness and confidence when learning or using digital tools and automated experiences.

Example: A confident user explores advanced settings alone, while a hesitant user requests guided setup.

docs.title: Theme Identification
docs.text: The process of developing broader recurring ideas from related coded observations while retaining links to source evidence.

docs.title: Traditional Focus Groups
docs.text:
Moderated conversations with recruited human participants who discuss a topic together and provide qualitative evidence about their perceptions.

Example: A human moderator leads eight recruited customers through a ninety-minute discussion of package designs.

docs.title: Trust Disposition
docs.text:
A person’s general tendency to trust or question organizations, claims, technology, and other people.

Example: One persona accepts verified customer reviews readily, while another demands independent documentation.

docs.title: Trust Score
docs.text: A rating of how safe, honest, reliable, and credible an asset appears to the intended audience.

docs.title: Trust Signal
docs.text: A visible cue, such as transparent terms, credible proof, or recognizable endorsement, that reduces perceived uncertainty.

docs.title: Turn Taking
docs.text:
The controlled order in which participants contribute, preventing interruption and preserving an interpretable discussion record.

See also: Debate Protocol, Discussion Agenda

docs.title: Typography Evaluation
docs.text: Assessment of type choices for legibility, hierarchy, personality, accessibility, and consistency across formats.

docs.title: Uncertainty Disclosure
docs.text:
Explicit communication of what is unknown, variable, assumed, or weakly supported in an analysis or recommendation.

Example: The summary states that simulated reactions suggest a risk but cannot estimate its population frequency.

docs.title: Use Context
docs.text:
The immediate environment, device, time pressure, and activity surrounding a customer’s interaction with an asset.

Example: The manager compares prices on a phone during a commute and later shares screenshots with the owner.

docs.title: User Prompt
docs.text:
The immediate request, question, or source material submitted for the AI system to address within higher-priority rules and available context.

Example: The marketer asks, “Compare these two headlines against the approved clarity rubric.”

docs.title: Value Proposition
docs.text: A concise explanation of the customer benefit an offering provides, for whom, and why it is preferable to alternatives.

docs.title: Value-Based Segmentation
docs.text:
Grouping customers by their economic value to the organization or by the value they seek from an offer.

Example: The team distinguishes high-lifetime-value accounts from low-frequency buyers for service planning.

docs.title: Video Evaluation
docs.text: Assessment of a video’s opening, narrative, pacing, sound, visuals, comprehension, brand fit, and call to action.

docs.title: Visual Design Critic
docs.text: An expert agent that examines hierarchy, composition, color, typography, imagery, and usability in a visual asset.

docs.title: Visual Identity
docs.text: The recognizable system of logos, colors, typography, imagery, and layout that visually represents a brand.

docs.title: Website Evaluation
docs.text: Assessment of a website’s messaging, navigation, usability, trust, accessibility, and ability to support visitor goals.

docs.title: Weighted Criteria
docs.text: Evaluation criteria assigned different influence on the total result according to their importance to the decision.

docs.title: Workflow Automation
docs.text:
The use of configured rules and tools to execute repeatable process steps with limited manual handling.

See also: No-Code Workflow, Evaluation Pipeline
