docs.title: Concept Taxonomy
docs.text: The 400 concepts are organized into 14 primary categories.

docs.title: AI And Research Foundations
docs.text:
TaxonomyID: AIFND

Core AI, language model, synthetic user, and customer research concepts. This category contains concepts 1–20.

docs.title: Research Design And AI Limits
docs.text:
TaxonomyID: RSRCH

Research methods, evidence quality, model limitations, and bias awareness. This category contains concepts 21–60.

docs.title: Ethics Privacy And Governance
docs.text:
TaxonomyID: ETHIC

Responsible use, privacy, fairness, transparency, oversight, and accountability. This category contains concepts 61–80.

docs.title: Persona Design
docs.text:
TaxonomyID: PERDS

Persona attributes, motivations, needs, behaviors, scenarios, and evidence bases. This category contains concepts 81–120.

docs.title: Persona Segmentation And Quality
docs.text:
TaxonomyID: PERQ

Segmentation, Jobs-to-be-Done, journeys, persona testing, consistency, and reuse. This category contains concepts 121–180.

docs.title: Prompt Design And Outputs
docs.text:
TaxonomyID: PRMPT

Prompt structure, context, roles, constraints, templates, and structured responses. This category contains concepts 181–220.

docs.title: Prompt Testing And Dialogue
docs.text:
TaxonomyID: PTEST

Prompt evaluation, failure modes, question design, and synthesis techniques. This category contains concepts 221–240.

docs.title: Agent Design And Roles
docs.text:
TaxonomyID: AGENT

Agent anatomy, boundaries, persona roles, and specialist reviewer roles. This category contains concepts 241–260.

docs.title: Multi-Agent Orchestration
docs.text:
TaxonomyID: MULTI

Coordination, discussion, debate, aggregation, consensus, and critique patterns. This category contains concepts 261–290.

docs.title: Brand And Asset Evaluation
docs.text:
TaxonomyID: BRAND

Brand strategy, messaging, identity, channels, assets, and customer journeys. This category contains concepts 291–320.

docs.title: Rubrics Scores And Evidence
docs.text:
TaxonomyID: RUBRC

Evaluation frameworks, scoring, evidence, confidence, and marketing criteria. This category contains concepts 321–350.

docs.title: Analysis And Recommendations
docs.text:
TaxonomyID: ANLYS

Pattern analysis, risk assessment, prioritization, and iterative improvement. This category contains concepts 351–370.

docs.title: Graph-Based Knowledge Models
docs.text:
TaxonomyID: GRAPH

Knowledge graph structures, relationships, evidence, and organizational memory. This category contains concepts 371–386.

docs.title: Automation Reporting And Capstone
docs.text:
TaxonomyID: WORKF

No-code workflows, automated reports, dashboards, and scalable systems. This category contains concepts 387–400.

docs.title: Course Description Assessment
docs.text: Course: AI Persona Testing for Marketing Professionals Assessment Date: 2026-07-03 Analyzer Version: 0.03

docs.title: Overall Score: 100/100
docs.text:
Quality Rating: ✅ Excellent — Ready for learning graph generation

This course description is exemplary in its completeness, clarity, and alignment with educational best practices.

docs.title: Detailed Scoring Breakdown
docs.text:
Element Points Status Notes Title 5/5 ✅ Clear, descriptive, audience-specific title Target Audience 5/5 ✅ Detailed list of 10 specific professional roles Prerequisites 5/5 ✅ Explicit "no programming required" + clear non-prerequisites Main Topics Covered 10/10 ✅ 8 major topic areas with subsections; comprehensive scope Topics Excluded 5/5 ✅ "Topics NOT Covered" section sets clear boundaries (10 excluded topics) Learning Outcomes Header 5/5 ✅ Clear framework: "Students will be able to..." using Bloom's Taxonomy Remember Level 10/10 ✅ 5 specific outcomes with measurable verbs (define, describe, identify, list, recall) Understand Level 10/10 ✅ 5 specific outcomes with clear verbs (explain, compare, summarize, describe) Apply Level 10/10 ✅ 5 specific outcomes with action verbs (create, construct, conduct, apply, generate) Analyze Level 10/10 ✅ 5 specific outcomes with analytical verbs (compare, differentiate, detect, analyze, identify) Evaluate Level 10/10 ✅ 5 specific outcomes with evaluative verbs (assess, judge, critique, prioritize, defend) Create Level 10/10 ✅ 6 specific outcomes with creative verbs (design, develop, create, build, produce, design scalable systems) Descriptive Context 5/5 ✅ Multiple sections provide rationale ("Why This Course Matters," "Course Overview," "Expected Outcomes")

Total: 100/100

docs.title: Strengths
docs.text:
1. Comprehensive Topic Coverage — The course spans eight interconnected domains (foundations, personas, prompt engineering, multi-agent systems, brand evaluation, structured evaluation, graph-based models, workflow automation) that collectively support 200+ concepts.

  2. All Six Bloom's Levels Fully Developed — Each taxonomy level has 5–6 specific, measurable outcomes. All outcomes use action verbs appropriate to their level.

  3. Explicit Scope Boundaries — The "Topics NOT Covered" section clearly delineates what is outside the course (programming, ML algorithms, data engineering), which helps the learning graph generator focus on the right concepts.

  4. Strong Capstone Project — The capstone integrates personas, evaluation rubrics, graph-based knowledge models, and reporting—ideal anchors for concept dependencies.

  5. Real-World Context — The course is framed around a practical problem (expensive, slow traditional research) and shows a concrete workflow diagram, making it relatable for the target audience.

  6. Weekly Structure — The 10-week outline maps to topic progression, which will help organize concepts chronologically and by prerequisite order.

  7. Non-Technical Audience Clarity — Repeatedly emphasizes "no programming required," setting clear expectations and defining the conceptual (not technical) scope.

  8. Graph-Based Knowledge Model Integration — Week 7 and the capstone project explicitly reference organizing evaluations as a graph structure (personas, assets, goals, pain points, emotional reactions, evaluation criteria, recommendations, evidence)—this directly supports concept-relationship mapping in the learning graph.

docs.title: Gap Analysis
docs.text:
No gaps identified. ✅

The course description contains all required elements at high quality: - Every Bloom's Taxonomy level has multiple, specific, measurable outcomes - Topics are well-defined and bounded - Audience, prerequisites, and context are explicit - The capstone project is substantive and integrated across all learning levels

docs.title: Concept Generation Readiness Assessment
docs.text:
Estimated Concept Count: 180–250 concepts Readiness Level: Excellent

Reasoning: - Breadth: 8 major topic areas, each containing 3–5 subtopics = ~30+ distinct conceptual domains - Depth: Bloom's Taxonomy spans from recall (foundational concepts) to creation (complex systems and workflows) - Integration: Graph-based organization, multi-agent systems, and workflow automation introduce cross-cutting concepts - Domain-Specificity: Marketing terminology (brand voice, messaging consistency, customer journey, purchase intent) adds 50–60 domain-specific concepts - Process Concepts: Prompt engineering, rubric design, workflow automation, and reporting introduce 40–60 procedural concepts - Analytical Concepts: Persona consistency, bias identification, confidence ratings, and evidence collection add 20–30 meta-analytical concepts

Recommendation: Proceed directly to learning-graph-generator. The course description contains sufficient detail and breadth to generate a high-quality 200-concept DAG.

docs.text:
1. ✅ Course description is ready — No revisions needed
  2. ✅ Metadata added to course-description.md — YAML frontmatter includes quality_score
  3. ⏭️ Ready for learning-graph-generator — Recommend running next to enumerate concepts and build the dependency graph

docs.title: Concept Categories (Preview)
docs.text:
The learning graph is likely to include concepts organized into categories such as:

  • Foundations & Theory: LLM basics, AI agents, synthetic personas, ethical considerations
  • Persona Design: Demographics, behavioral segmentation, Jobs-to-be-Done, motivation, bias
  • Prompt Engineering: Consistency, context management, structured outputs, testing
  • Multi-Agent Orchestration: Moderator patterns, consensus, debate, skeptic agents, expert reviewers
  • Brand Evaluation: Logos, websites, advertisements, campaigns, voice, messaging, customer journeys
  • Structured Evaluation: Rubrics, scoring systems, confidence ratings, evidence collection, prioritization
  • Graph Representations: Nodes (personas, assets, goals, pain points, criteria), edges (relationships), organization
  • Workflow & Automation: Pipelines, orchestration, reporting, reproducibility
  • Marketing Metrics: Trust, clarity, emotional resonance, memorability, purchase intent, differentiation
  • Analysis & Improvement: Pattern detection, inconsistency detection, recommendation synthesis, risk assessment

docs.title: Full Version
docs.text:
I’m excited to share AI Persona Testing, a free interactive intelligent textbook for marketing professionals—and a milestone in how we build open educational resources.

This is the first full interactive intelligent textbook we have generated with an OpenAI model rather than Anthropic Claude. Built with OpenAI’s newest GPT-5.5 model—available in ChatGPT and Codex—using the Codex desktop app, it gave us a valuable test of whether our skill-driven textbook workflow could transfer cleanly across model providers while preserving structure, pedagogy, and quality.

The book teaches marketers, brand strategists, UX researchers, consultants, and business leaders how to design repeatable AI persona testing systems without programming. It covers evidence quality, responsible AI, persona design, prompt testing, multi-agent workflows, evaluation rubrics, knowledge graphs, and automated reporting.

📊 By the numbers: • 20 chapters • 400 concepts organized in a prerequisite-aware learning graph • 82 interactive MicroSims and 81 diagrams • 200 quiz questions across 20 chapter quizzes • 400 glossary terms and 82 FAQs • 153,530 words—about 675 printed pages

The larger lesson is encouraging: a carefully designed system of reusable skills, source-of-truth metrics, validation checks, and human direction can make an intelligent-textbook workflow portable across frontier models. The model matters, but so does the process wrapped around it.

The full textbook is free to explore. Link in the first comment below. 👇

docs.title: Medium Version
docs.text:
I’m excited to share AI Persona Testing, a free interactive intelligent textbook for marketing professionals.

This is the first full interactive intelligent textbook we have generated with an OpenAI model rather than Anthropic Claude. We built it with OpenAI’s newest GPT-5.5 model—available in ChatGPT and Codex—using the Codex desktop app, testing whether our reusable, skill-driven textbook workflow could transfer across model providers without losing pedagogical structure or quality.

📊 The finished book includes: • 20 chapters and 400 learning-graph concepts • 82 interactive MicroSims and 81 diagrams • 200 quiz questions • 400 glossary terms and 82 FAQs • 153,530 words—about 675 printed pages

It teaches marketers how to design responsible, repeatable AI persona testing systems without programming.

Explore the free textbook via the link in the first comment. 👇

docs.title: Lens Mascot Style Guide
docs.text:
This page previews every Lens mascot pose and admonition style. Lens is the book's evidence-focused research guide—not a customer persona or a source of customer evidence.

A Note from Lens - Neutral

Keep observations, assumptions, and conclusions separate so another researcher can follow your reasoning.

Let's Look Closer - Welcome

Start with the decision you need to support. Then choose the evidence and evaluation method that fit that decision.

Key Insight - Thinking

A plausible persona response is still a model output. What additional evidence would you need before making a claim about real customers?

Lens's Research Tip - Tip

Save the asset, prompt, rubric, model settings, and run date together. That small habit makes later comparisons much easier to interpret.

Pause and Check - Warning

Do not present synthetic reactions as customer evidence. Use them to form hypotheses that appropriate research with real people can examine.

Keep Investigating - Encouraging

A failed prompt test is useful evidence. Record the failure, revise one variable, and run the comparison again.

Evidence Secured - Celebration

You connected the recommendation to its evidence and documented the uncertainty. That is meaningful research progress.

docs.title: MicroSims
docs.text:
MicroSims are small, interactive educational simulations — each one focused on a single concept. They live under docs/sims/<sim-name>/ and are embedded into chapters via iframes.

New MicroSims can be created with the microsim-generator skill, which routes to the appropriate library (p5.js, Chart.js, vis-network, Mermaid, Leaflet, Plotly, Venn.js).

docs.title: About This MicroSim
docs.text: Assess an agent action by consequence, reversibility, data sensitivity, tool scope, and required human approval.

docs.title: How to Use
docs.text: Explore the visual elements and use the controls to compare states, reveal guidance, and explain how the displayed evidence supports or limits a decision. Values shown are instructional examples unless the chapter identifies an empirical source.

docs.title: Iframe Embed Code
docs.text: <iframe src="https://dmccreary.github.io/ai-persona-testing/sims/agent-autonomy-boundary/main.html" height="702" width="100%" scrolling="no"></iframe>

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Assess an agent action by consequence, reversibility, data sensitivity, tool scope, and required human approval.

docs.title: Activity
docs.text:
1. Predict what the first interaction will reveal.
  2. Explore each state and cite visible evidence.
  3. Apply the model to a new marketing scenario and identify its limits.

docs.title: Assessment
docs.text: Learners should perform the Create task in the objective and distinguish synthetic output from externally validated evidence.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
1. Chapter source - Full instructional context and specification.
  2. p5.js documentation - Rendering-library reference.

docs.title: Learning Graph Viewer
docs.text: This interactive viewer allows you to explore the learning graph for this course.

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• Search: Type in the search box to find specific concepts
  • Category Filtering: Use checkboxes to show/hide concept categories
  • Interactive Navigation: Click and drag to explore, scroll to zoom
  • Statistics: View real-time counts of visible nodes and edges

docs.title: Using the Viewer
docs.text:
1. Search for Concepts: Start typing in the search box to find concepts. Click on a result to focus on that node.

  2. Filter by Category: Use the category checkboxes in the sidebar to show or hide groups of related concepts. Use "Check All" or "Uncheck All" for bulk operations.

  3. Navigate the Graph:

  4. Drag to pan around the graph
  5. Scroll to zoom in and out

  6. Click on a node to select it and highlight its connections

  7. View Statistics: The sidebar shows counts of visible nodes, edges, and foundational concepts.

docs.title: Graph Structure
docs.text:
• Foundational Concepts (left side): Prerequisites with no dependencies
  • Advanced Concepts (right side): Topics that build on multiple prerequisites
  • Edges: Arrows point from a concept to its prerequisites

docs.title: Launch the Viewer
docs.text: Open Learning Graph Viewer

docs.title: Language Model Response Laboratory
docs.text: Run the Language Model Response Laboratory MicroSim Fullscreen Edit in the p5.js Editor

docs.title: About This MicroSim
docs.text: This worked example makes four otherwise abstract ideas visible: instructional roles, simplified tokens, finite context, and illustrative next-phrase probabilities. It explicitly distinguishes stable meaning from variable wording without claiming access to proprietary model internals.

docs.title: How to Use
docs.text: Make a prediction, then use Next and Previous to inspect each stage. At the context stage, add irrelevant material; at the probability stage, vary the illustrative distribution and compare the two final responses.

docs.title: Iframe Embed Code
docs.text:
You can add this MicroSim to any web page by adding this to your HTML:

<iframe src="https://dmccreary.github.io/ai-persona-testing/sims/language-model-response-lab/main.html"
        height="717"
        width="100%"
        scrolling="no"></iframe>

docs.title: Grade Level
docs.text: High school, undergraduate, and professional AI literacy

docs.title: Duration
docs.text: 10-15 minutes

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: No programming experience is required; learners should understand that language models generate text from prompts.

docs.title: Activities
docs.text:
1. Predict (3 min): Choose which input will most influence the response.
  2. Step through (7 min): Explain what changes at each processing stage.
  3. Compare (5 min): Identify stable meaning and variable wording across two runs.

docs.title: Assessment
docs.text: Learners can explain the role of instructions, tokenization, context limits, and probabilistic selection without treating the teaching probabilities as actual internal model values.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
1. Attention Is All You Need - Foundational transformer architecture paper.
  2. p5.js Reference - Documentation for the interactive rendering library.

docs.title: Persona Simulation Mode Explorer
docs.text: Run the Persona Simulation Mode Explorer MicroSim Fullscreen Edit in the p5.js Editor

docs.title: About This MicroSim
docs.text: This explorer differentiates customer simulation, agent-based simulation, AI focus groups, and brand stress testing. Selecting a mode redraws the participant structure and explains the evidence the mode can reasonably produce.

docs.title: How to Use
docs.text: Select or click a mode, pin two modes for comparison, then choose a research scenario and test which mode best fits. Read the structural and evidence explanation rather than choosing by name alone.

docs.title: Iframe Embed Code
docs.text:
You can add this MicroSim to any web page by adding this to your HTML:

<iframe src="https://dmccreary.github.io/ai-persona-testing/sims/persona-simulation-mode-explorer/main.html"
        height="712"
        width="100%"
        scrolling="no"></iframe>

docs.title: Grade Level
docs.text: Undergraduate and professional marketing research

docs.title: Duration
docs.text: 10-15 minutes

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: Basic familiarity with personas, customer research, and research objectives.

docs.title: Activities
docs.text:
1. Explore (4 min): Inspect all four participant structures.
  2. Compare (5 min): Pin two modes and explain how their evidence differs.
  3. Apply (5 min): Match each scenario to the best mode and defend the choice.

docs.title: Assessment
docs.text: Learners can differentiate the four modes using purpose, number of agents, interaction pattern, and expected evidence.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
1. The Surprising Power of Online Experiments - Background on disciplined testing and evidence.
  2. p5.js Reference - Documentation for the interactive rendering library.

docs.title: AI Persona Testing Evidence Workflow
docs.text: Run the AI Persona Testing Evidence Workflow MicroSim Fullscreen Edit in the p5.js Editor

docs.title: About This MicroSim
docs.text: This three-lane workflow separates marketing-team inputs, persona-system analysis, and human-research validation. Each stage exposes its input, action, output, and quality check so synthetic hypotheses do not quietly become validated claims.

docs.title: How to Use
docs.text: Choose a scenario, inspect each workflow step, then place evidence artifacts into their proper stages. Incorrect placements explain the correct handoff; completed stages turn green.

docs.title: Iframe Embed Code
docs.text:
You can add this MicroSim to any web page by adding this to your HTML:

<iframe src="https://dmccreary.github.io/ai-persona-testing/sims/persona-testing-evidence-workflow/main.html"
        height="752"
        width="100%"
        scrolling="no"></iframe>

docs.title: Grade Level
docs.text: Undergraduate and professional marketing research

docs.title: Duration
docs.text: 10-15 minutes

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: Basic familiarity with customer personas, qualitative research, and evidence limitations.

docs.title: Activities
docs.text:
1. Trace (4 min): Click through the nine stages and name each handoff.
  2. Practice (7 min): Place all evidence artifacts for one scenario.
  3. Transfer (5 min): Explain which synthetic claims require real-customer validation and why.

docs.title: Assessment
docs.text: Learners correctly place the artifacts and produce a validation decision that clearly distinguishes synthetic hypotheses from externally verified evidence.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
1. The Craft of Research - Guidance on evidence, claims, and research design.
  2. p5.js Reference - Documentation for the interactive rendering library.

docs.title: Research Question Method Navigator
docs.text: Run the Research Question Method Navigator MicroSim Fullscreen Edit in the p5.js Editor

docs.title: About This MicroSim
docs.text: This MicroSim helps learners distinguish customer research from market research and qualitative evidence from quantitative evidence. Ten realistic marketing questions reveal why a method fits and when a mixed-method extension would be stronger.

docs.title: How to Use
docs.text: Read the question, select the method that would produce the strongest first evidence, and choose Check classification. Use Show another defensible answer to examine ambiguity, then continue to the next question.

docs.title: Iframe Embed Code
docs.text:
You can add this MicroSim to any web page by adding this to your HTML:

<iframe src="https://dmccreary.github.io/ai-persona-testing/sims/research-method-navigator/main.html"
        height="652"
        width="100%"
        scrolling="no"></iframe>

docs.title: Grade Level
docs.text: Undergraduate and professional marketing education

docs.title: Duration
docs.text: 10-15 minutes

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: Basic familiarity with qualitative and quantitative research.

docs.title: Activities
docs.text:
1. Predict (3 min): Classify the first three questions before requesting feedback.
  2. Practice (7 min): Complete the remaining scenarios and explain what evidence each method would produce.
  3. Reflect (5 min): Select two ambiguous questions and propose mixed-method sequences.

docs.title: Assessment
docs.text: Learners should correctly classify at least eight scenarios and justify choices using the evidence needed rather than the surface wording of the question.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
1. The Craft of Research - University of Chicago Press guidance on aligning questions, evidence, and claims.
  2. p5.js Reference - Documentation for the interactive rendering library.
